title: AP World History: Modern MCQ Practice Exam 08

questions.prompt: Originalized oral praise text, Ife, West Africa, c. 1300: “The oni sits where the sacred grove meets the market road. Bronze workers cast the ruler’s calm face, priests guard the old rites, and traders bring beads and cloth to the city gates.” What was the most likely purpose of this praise text?

choices.A: To show that Ife was governed by foreign merchants
choices.B: To argue that West African cities lacked artistic production
choices.C: To present the ruler as a sacred and political figure connected to urban prosperity
choices.D: To describe an industrial factory town

questions.correctAnswerText: To present the ruler as a sacred and political figure connected to urban prosperity
questions.explanation: The praise text links the oni to sacred space, market roads, artisans, priests, and traders.

questions.prompt: Originalized oral praise text, Ife, West Africa, c. 1300: “The oni sits where the sacred grove meets the market road. Bronze workers cast the ruler’s calm face, priests guard the old rites, and traders bring beads and cloth to the city gates.” Which phrase best supports the claim that religion was linked to political authority?

choices.A: “The sacred grove meets the market road”
choices.B: “Traders bring beads”
choices.C: “Bronze workers cast”
choices.D: “City gates”

questions.correctAnswerText: “The sacred grove meets the market road”
questions.explanation: This phrase connects sacred geography with the ruler’s urban and political setting.

questions.prompt: Originalized oral praise text, Ife, West Africa, c. 1300: “The oni sits where the sacred grove meets the market road. Bronze workers cast the ruler’s calm face, priests guard the old rites, and traders bring beads and cloth to the city gates.” Which material object would best illustrate the political culture described?

choices.A: A steam locomotive built for export mining
choices.B: A printed revolutionary newspaper
choices.C: A porcelain plate from an East Asian kiln
choices.D: A naturalistic metal head representing royal authority

questions.correctAnswerText: A naturalistic metal head representing royal authority
questions.explanation: Ife is known for sophisticated metal portraiture associated with royal and elite authority.

questions.prompt: Originalized oral praise text, Ife, West Africa, c. 1300: “The oni sits where the sacred grove meets the market road. Bronze workers cast the ruler’s calm face, priests guard the old rites, and traders bring beads and cloth to the city gates.” The passage most directly suggests which feature of Ife’s society?

choices.A: The absence of social specialization
choices.B: The interaction of rulers, priests, artisans, and merchants in an urban center
choices.C: The replacement of local rulers by European governors
choices.D: The disappearance of craft production before 1450

questions.correctAnswerText: The interaction of rulers, priests, artisans, and merchants in an urban center
questions.explanation: The text describes the oni, priests, bronze workers, traders, and the city gates.

questions.prompt: Secondary-source description of Kilwa, c. 1300: “Stone mosques, coral-built houses, and coinage bearing local rulers’ names appeared in a coastal city whose elites claimed Islamic status while governing a Swahili-speaking population.” Which broader context best explains Kilwa’s growth?

choices.A: Swahili city-states combined local African cultures, Islamic influence, and coastal commerce.
choices.B: Kilwa was founded by Portuguese settlers after 1500.
choices.C: East African coastal cities rejected all overseas exchange.
choices.D: Swahili rulers depended on industrial wage labor.

questions.correctAnswerText: Swahili city-states combined local African cultures, Islamic influence, and coastal commerce.
questions.explanation: Kilwa’s mosques, local coinage, rulers, and Swahili-speaking population show local and Islamic coastal urban culture.

questions.prompt: Secondary-source description of Kilwa, c. 1300: “Stone mosques, coral-built houses, and coinage bearing local rulers’ names appeared in a coastal city whose elites claimed Islamic status while governing a Swahili-speaking population.” Which evidence best supports the claim that Islamic culture shaped coastal urban life?

choices.A: “Coral-built houses”
choices.B: “Coinage bearing local rulers’ names”
choices.C: “Swahili-speaking population”
choices.D: “Stone mosques”

questions.correctAnswerText: “Stone mosques”
questions.explanation: Stone mosques most directly show Islamic influence in coastal urban life.

questions.prompt: Secondary-source description of Kilwa, c. 1300: “Stone mosques, coral-built houses, and coinage bearing local rulers’ names appeared in a coastal city whose elites claimed Islamic status while governing a Swahili-speaking population.” Kilwa’s coinage most directly reflects which development?

choices.A: The decline of monetized exchange in East Africa
choices.B: The use of local political authority to support regional and oceanic commerce
choices.C: The replacement of coastal trade by inland subsistence farming
choices.D: The end of elite involvement in commerce

questions.correctAnswerText: The use of local political authority to support regional and oceanic commerce
questions.explanation: Coinage bearing local rulers’ names reflects political authority connected to commerce.

questions.prompt: Secondary-source description of Kilwa, c. 1300: “Stone mosques, coral-built houses, and coinage bearing local rulers’ names appeared in a coastal city whose elites claimed Islamic status while governing a Swahili-speaking population.” The description most directly illustrates which effect of Indian Ocean exchange?

choices.A: The disappearance of local African languages
choices.B: The creation of societies combining local identities with wider Islamic commercial culture
choices.C: The elimination of coastal cities after 1200
choices.D: The spread of Buddhism as the main religion of East Africa

questions.correctAnswerText: The creation of societies combining local identities with wider Islamic commercial culture
questions.explanation: Kilwa combined Swahili language and local rule with Islamic status and Indian Ocean commerce.

questions.prompt: Described map: “A map of the Black Sea c. 1300 shows routes linking Crimea, Constantinople, Trebizond, and Italian merchant colonies. Arrows mark grain, wax, furs, and enslaved people moving through ports into Mediterranean markets.” Which conclusion is best supported by the map?

choices.A: Black Sea routes linked Eurasian frontier zones to Mediterranean commercial networks.
choices.B: The Black Sea was isolated from long-distance trade.
choices.C: Italian merchants avoided all commercial activity east of the Mediterranean.
choices.D: Grain and furs moved only through overland Saharan routes.

questions.correctAnswerText: Black Sea routes linked Eurasian frontier zones to Mediterranean commercial networks.
questions.explanation: The map shows Crimea, Constantinople, Trebizond, Italian colonies, and commodities moving into Mediterranean markets.

questions.prompt: Described map: “A map of the Black Sea c. 1300 shows routes linking Crimea, Constantinople, Trebizond, and Italian merchant colonies. Arrows mark grain, wax, furs, and enslaved people moving through ports into Mediterranean markets.” Which audience would most likely have used a map like this?

choices.A: Factory owners planning rail schedules in colonial India
choices.B: Merchants and officials organizing port duties, convoy routes, and commercial privileges
choices.C: Women petitioning revolutionary assemblies for citizenship
choices.D: United Nations observers during a twentieth-century genocide

questions.correctAnswerText: Merchants and officials organizing port duties, convoy routes, and commercial privileges
questions.explanation: The map would be useful to people coordinating maritime trade, taxes, and privileges.

questions.prompt: Originalized decree from the Deccan Sultanate of Bijapur, c. 1650: “The fort commanders shall store grain and gunpowder before the dry season. Horse dealers from the western sea must pay duties at the city gate, and poets shall praise the sultan’s justice in Persian and Dakhani verse.” What was the most likely purpose of the decree?

choices.A: To abolish all military forts in the Deccan
choices.B: To place Bijapur under Portuguese rule
choices.C: To end the use of Persianate court culture
choices.D: To coordinate military defense, revenue collection, and court legitimacy

questions.correctAnswerText: To coordinate military defense, revenue collection, and court legitimacy
questions.explanation: The decree covers forts, gunpowder, horse duties, city gates, poetry, and sultanic justice.

questions.prompt: Originalized decree from the Deccan Sultanate of Bijapur, c. 1650: “The fort commanders shall store grain and gunpowder before the dry season. Horse dealers from the western sea must pay duties at the city gate, and poets shall praise the sultan’s justice in Persian and Dakhani verse.” Which phrase best supports the claim that military technology shaped state policy?

choices.A: “Store grain and gunpowder”
choices.B: “Horse dealers”
choices.C: “Poets shall praise”
choices.D: “City gate”

questions.correctAnswerText: “Store grain and gunpowder”
questions.explanation: Gunpowder storage directly shows military technology shaping policy.

questions.prompt: Originalized decree from the Deccan Sultanate of Bijapur, c. 1650: “The fort commanders shall store grain and gunpowder before the dry season. Horse dealers from the western sea must pay duties at the city gate, and poets shall praise the sultan’s justice in Persian and Dakhani verse.” Which broader cultural context best explains the reference to Persian and Dakhani verse?

choices.A: The Deccan courts blended Persianate literary traditions with regional South Asian languages.
choices.B: The Deccan sultanates rejected court poetry as politically dangerous.
choices.C: Persian verse was used only in European parliaments.
choices.D: Dakhani was a language created by industrial schools after 1900.

questions.correctAnswerText: The Deccan courts blended Persianate literary traditions with regional South Asian languages.
questions.explanation: Persian and Dakhani verse together show cultural blending in Deccan court culture.

questions.prompt: Originalized decree from the Deccan Sultanate of Bijapur, c. 1650: “The fort commanders shall store grain and gunpowder before the dry season. Horse dealers from the western sea must pay duties at the city gate, and poets shall praise the sultan’s justice in Persian and Dakhani verse.” The reference to horse dealers from the western sea most directly reflects which connection?

choices.A: The isolation of Deccan states from maritime exchange
choices.B: The role of overseas and regional trade in supplying military needs
choices.C: The decline of cavalry in South Asia before 1450
choices.D: The replacement of trade duties by income taxes

questions.correctAnswerText: The role of overseas and regional trade in supplying military needs
questions.explanation: Horse dealers from the western sea show maritime and regional trade supporting cavalry warfare.

questions.prompt: Described map: “A map of the Toungoo Empire, mainland Southeast Asia, c. 1550–1600, shows fortified centers along river valleys, routes through forested uplands, and arrows marking campaigns toward rice plains and port-linked regions.” Which conclusion is best supported by the map?

choices.A: Toungoo expansion was unrelated to river valleys or agricultural regions.
choices.B: Mainland Southeast Asian empires expanded by combining inland routes, fortified centers, and control over agrarian zones.
choices.C: The Toungoo Empire depended entirely on Atlantic plantation commerce.
choices.D: River valleys prevented political expansion in mainland Southeast Asia.

questions.correctAnswerText: Mainland Southeast Asian empires expanded by combining inland routes, fortified centers, and control over agrarian zones.
questions.explanation: The map shows fortified centers, river valleys, upland routes, rice plains, and port-linked regions.

questions.prompt: Described map: “A map of the Toungoo Empire, mainland Southeast Asia, c. 1550–1600, shows fortified centers along river valleys, routes through forested uplands, and arrows marking campaigns toward rice plains and port-linked regions.” Which map feature best supports the claim that military expansion and resource control were connected?

choices.A: Arrows marking campaigns toward rice plains and port-linked regions
choices.B: The label “mainland Southeast Asia”
choices.C: Forested uplands
choices.D: The date c. 1550–1600

questions.correctAnswerText: Arrows marking campaigns toward rice plains and port-linked regions
questions.explanation: These arrows directly connect military campaigns with economically valuable regions.

questions.prompt: Described map: “A map of the Toungoo Empire, mainland Southeast Asia, c. 1550–1600, shows fortified centers along river valleys, routes through forested uplands, and arrows marking campaigns toward rice plains and port-linked regions.” Which argument is best supported by the map?

choices.A: Mainland empires could link warfare, geography, and agrarian production in state-building.
choices.B: Southeast Asian empires avoided control over rice-producing areas.
choices.C: Port-linked regions had no political value to inland rulers.
choices.D: Fortified centers were used only by European maritime companies.

questions.correctAnswerText: Mainland empires could link warfare, geography, and agrarian production in state-building.
questions.explanation: The map links campaigns, river valleys, forts, rice plains, and port-linked areas.

questions.prompt: Originalized English East India Company letter from Surat, c. 1620: “The local governor demands customs before our cloth may be sold. We must send gifts to secure the warehouse lease, yet Gujarati brokers know the buyers better than any factor newly arrived from London.” What was the most likely purpose of the letter?

choices.A: To argue that European companies controlled Indian commerce without local cooperation
choices.B: To report the practical challenges of trading within an established Asian commercial system
choices.C: To announce the end of textile commerce in India
choices.D: To request the abolition of all customs duties in Europe

questions.correctAnswerText: To report the practical challenges of trading within an established Asian commercial system
questions.explanation: The letter mentions local customs, warehouse leases, gifts, Gujarati brokers, and London factors.

questions.prompt: Originalized English East India Company letter from Surat, c. 1620: “The local governor demands customs before our cloth may be sold. We must send gifts to secure the warehouse lease, yet Gujarati brokers know the buyers better than any factor newly arrived from London.” Which phrase best supports the claim that local political authority constrained European merchants?

choices.A: “The local governor demands customs”
choices.B: “Our cloth”
choices.C: “London”
choices.D: “Gujarati brokers”

questions.correctAnswerText: “The local governor demands customs”
questions.explanation: This phrase shows local political authority regulating European trade.

questions.prompt: Originalized English East India Company letter from Surat, c. 1620: “The local governor demands customs before our cloth may be sold. We must send gifts to secure the warehouse lease, yet Gujarati brokers know the buyers better than any factor newly arrived from London.” Which broader context best explains the role of Gujarati brokers?

choices.A: Indian Ocean trade involved experienced local merchant communities before European companies arrived.
choices.B: European companies eliminated all Asian merchants by 1600.
choices.C: Gujarati brokers were agents of the Spanish monarchy in Mexico.
choices.D: South Asian textile commerce began only after British industrialization.

questions.correctAnswerText: Indian Ocean trade involved experienced local merchant communities before European companies arrived.
questions.explanation: Gujarati brokers’ knowledge of buyers reflects established local commercial networks.

questions.prompt: Originalized English East India Company letter from Surat, c. 1620: “The local governor demands customs before our cloth may be sold. We must send gifts to secure the warehouse lease, yet Gujarati brokers know the buyers better than any factor newly arrived from London.” Which statement best evaluates the commercial situation described?

choices.A: European merchants immediately dominated South Asian trade without negotiation.
choices.B: European companies relied on local officials, leases, brokers, and established markets to operate in Asian ports.
choices.C: Indian merchants withdrew from trade after European arrival.
choices.D: Company factories functioned as independent sovereign states in 1620.

questions.correctAnswerText: European companies relied on local officials, leases, brokers, and established markets to operate in Asian ports.
questions.explanation: The letter shows European dependence on local political and commercial systems.

questions.prompt: Described map: “A map of southern Chile c. 1550–1650 shows Spanish towns north of the Biobío River, Mapuche-controlled territories to the south, frontier forts, and arrows marking raids, negotiations, and prisoner exchanges.” Which conclusion is best supported by the map?

choices.A: The Mapuche maintained resistance and frontier diplomacy against Spanish expansion.
choices.B: Spanish rule quickly eliminated all Indigenous political autonomy in southern Chile.
choices.C: The Biobío frontier was unrelated to conflict or negotiation.
choices.D: Mapuche territory was governed directly from Madrid after 1550.

questions.correctAnswerText: The Mapuche maintained resistance and frontier diplomacy against Spanish expansion.
questions.explanation: The map shows Spanish towns, Mapuche-controlled territories, forts, raids, negotiations, and prisoner exchanges.

questions.prompt: Described map: “A map of southern Chile c. 1550–1650 shows Spanish towns north of the Biobío River, Mapuche-controlled territories to the south, frontier forts, and arrows marking raids, negotiations, and prisoner exchanges.” Which map feature best supports the claim that the frontier involved both conflict and diplomacy?

choices.A: Spanish towns north of the river
choices.B: The coastline of southern Chile
choices.C: Arrows marking raids, negotiations, and prisoner exchanges
choices.D: Mapuche-controlled territory

questions.correctAnswerText: Arrows marking raids, negotiations, and prisoner exchanges
questions.explanation: These arrows directly show both conflict and diplomacy.

questions.prompt: Described map: “A map of southern Chile c. 1550–1650 shows Spanish towns north of the Biobío River, Mapuche-controlled territories to the south, frontier forts, and arrows marking raids, negotiations, and prisoner exchanges.” The pattern shown most directly illustrates which broader feature of European expansion in the Americas?

choices.A: Indigenous peoples always lost political autonomy immediately.
choices.B: Frontier zones could involve prolonged Indigenous resistance, adaptation, and negotiated boundaries.
choices.C: European colonization occurred without military conflict.
choices.D: Indigenous communities had no role in shaping colonial frontiers.

questions.correctAnswerText: Frontier zones could involve prolonged Indigenous resistance, adaptation, and negotiated boundaries.
questions.explanation: The Biobío frontier shows long-term resistance and negotiation rather than immediate conquest.

questions.prompt: Originalized speech by a German liberal nationalist, Frankfurt, 1848: “A people divided into jealous courts cannot defend liberty. Let a parliament write rights into law, let censorship fall, and let Germans be citizens of a nation rather than subjects of princes.” Which audience was the speech most likely intended to influence?

choices.A: Merchants seeking access to Indian Ocean ports
choices.B: Delegates and readers debating constitutional nationalism in German lands
choices.C: Plantation overseers defending coerced labor
choices.D: Cold War officials negotiating a proxy conflict

questions.correctAnswerText: Delegates and readers debating constitutional nationalism in German lands
questions.explanation: The speech addresses parliament, rights, censorship, citizenship, nationhood, and princes.

questions.prompt: Originalized speech by a German liberal nationalist, Frankfurt, 1848: “A people divided into jealous courts cannot defend liberty. Let a parliament write rights into law, let censorship fall, and let Germans be citizens of a nation rather than subjects of princes.” Which phrase best supports the claim that the speaker connected nationalism to constitutional rights?

choices.A: “A people divided”
choices.B: “Parliament write rights into law”
choices.C: “Subjects of princes”
choices.D: “Jealous courts”

questions.correctAnswerText: “Parliament write rights into law”
questions.explanation: The phrase directly links constitutional institutions to rights.

questions.prompt: Originalized speech by a German liberal nationalist, Frankfurt, 1848: “A people divided into jealous courts cannot defend liberty. Let a parliament write rights into law, let censorship fall, and let Germans be citizens of a nation rather than subjects of princes.” Which broader context best explains the speech?

choices.A: The spread of liberal and nationalist movements during the revolutions of 1848
choices.B: The disappearance of print politics in nineteenth-century Europe
choices.C: The restoration of feudal serfdom across all German states
choices.D: The rise of decolonization movements in Southeast Asia

questions.correctAnswerText: The spread of liberal and nationalist movements during the revolutions of 1848
questions.explanation: The speech reflects the 1848 wave of liberal nationalism and constitutional demands.

questions.prompt: Originalized speech by a German liberal nationalist, Frankfurt, 1848: “A people divided into jealous courts cannot defend liberty. Let a parliament write rights into law, let censorship fall, and let Germans be citizens of a nation rather than subjects of princes.” Which statement best evaluates the speaker’s argument?

choices.A: The speaker defends dynastic fragmentation as the best protection for liberty.
choices.B: The speaker argues that national unity, constitutional government, and civil rights should reinforce one another.
choices.C: The speaker rejects citizenship in favor of hereditary subjecthood.
choices.D: The speaker claims censorship is necessary for political freedom.

questions.correctAnswerText: The speaker argues that national unity, constitutional government, and civil rights should reinforce one another.
questions.explanation: The speaker connects unity, parliament, rights, censorship, citizenship, and opposition to princely rule.

questions.prompt: Originalized proclamation from Cuban rebels, 1868: “The island cannot be free while men and women labor in chains. We call on planters, free people of color, and enslaved workers to join a nation where liberty is not reserved for those born white or wealthy.” What was the most likely purpose of the proclamation?

choices.A: To defend Spanish colonial rule and plantation slavery
choices.B: To separate independence from questions of race and labor
choices.C: To mobilize support for independence by linking nationalism and abolition
choices.D: To abolish all export agriculture immediately

questions.correctAnswerText: To mobilize support for independence by linking nationalism and abolition
questions.explanation: The proclamation links Cuban national freedom to ending slavery and broadening liberty.

questions.prompt: Originalized proclamation from Cuban rebels, 1868: “The island cannot be free while men and women labor in chains. We call on planters, free people of color, and enslaved workers to join a nation where liberty is not reserved for those born white or wealthy.” Which phrase best supports the claim that the proclamation challenged racial hierarchy?

choices.A: “The island cannot be free”
choices.B: “Planters”
choices.C: “Liberty is not reserved for those born white or wealthy”
choices.D: “Join a nation”

questions.correctAnswerText: “Liberty is not reserved for those born white or wealthy”
questions.explanation: This phrase directly challenges racial and class hierarchy.

questions.prompt: Originalized proclamation from Cuban rebels, 1868: “The island cannot be free while men and women labor in chains. We call on planters, free people of color, and enslaved workers to join a nation where liberty is not reserved for those born white or wealthy.” The proclamation most directly connects political revolution to which economic institution?

choices.A: Chattel slavery in a plantation economy
choices.B: Industrial wage labor in textile mills
choices.C: Caravan taxation in the Sahara
choices.D: State ownership of oil resources

questions.correctAnswerText: Chattel slavery in a plantation economy
questions.explanation: The reference to people laboring in chains in Cuba points to plantation slavery.

questions.prompt: Originalized proclamation from Cuban rebels, 1868: “The island cannot be free while men and women labor in chains. We call on planters, free people of color, and enslaved workers to join a nation where liberty is not reserved for those born white or wealthy.” Which statement best evaluates the proclamation’s argument?

choices.A: It treats slavery as unrelated to national independence.
choices.B: It defines liberty as compatible with racial exclusion.
choices.C: It rejects the participation of enslaved workers in politics.
choices.D: It argues that national freedom must include emancipation and a broader definition of citizenship.

questions.correctAnswerText: It argues that national freedom must include emancipation and a broader definition of citizenship.
questions.explanation: The proclamation defines independence as incompatible with slavery and racial exclusion.

questions.prompt:
Table: Treaty-port trade in China after the First Opium War, simplified pattern
Feature | Reported change
Ports open to foreign merchants | Increased from one main legal port to several treaty ports
Tariff control | Limited by treaty agreements
Foreign residence | Allowed in designated port districts
Which conclusion is best supported by the table?

choices.A: Treaty arrangements expanded foreign commercial access and weakened Qing control over trade policy.
choices.B: China gained full tariff autonomy after the First Opium War.
choices.C: Foreign merchants were banned from all Chinese ports after 1842.
choices.D: Treaty ports ended European interest in Chinese markets.

questions.correctAnswerText: Treaty arrangements expanded foreign commercial access and weakened Qing control over trade policy.
questions.explanation: More treaty ports, limited tariff control, and foreign residence show restricted Qing sovereignty.

questions.prompt:
Table: Treaty-port trade in China after the First Opium War, simplified pattern
Feature | Reported change
Ports open to foreign merchants | Increased from one main legal port to several treaty ports
Tariff control | Limited by treaty agreements
Foreign residence | Allowed in designated port districts
Which evidence best supports the claim that Qing sovereignty over trade was restricted?

choices.A: “Several treaty ports”
choices.B: “Limited by treaty agreements”
choices.C: “Foreign residence”
choices.D: “Ports open”

questions.correctAnswerText: “Limited by treaty agreements”
questions.explanation: Limitation of tariff control directly shows restricted sovereignty.

questions.prompt:
Table: Treaty-port trade in China after the First Opium War, simplified pattern
Feature | Reported change
Ports open to foreign merchants | Increased from one main legal port to several treaty ports
Tariff control | Limited by treaty agreements
Foreign residence | Allowed in designated port districts
Which broader context best explains the treaty-port system?

choices.A: European states and merchants used industrial-era military and naval power to force commercial concessions.
choices.B: Qing rulers invited foreign merchants to govern inland provinces freely.
choices.C: Chinese trade policy was unaffected by imperial conflict.
choices.D: Treaty ports were created by anticolonial independence movements.

questions.correctAnswerText: European states and merchants used industrial-era military and naval power to force commercial concessions.
questions.explanation: The treaty-port system followed military defeat and unequal treaties.

questions.prompt:
Table: Treaty-port trade in China after the First Opium War, simplified pattern
Feature | Reported change
Ports open to foreign merchants | Increased from one main legal port to several treaty ports
Tariff control | Limited by treaty agreements
Foreign residence | Allowed in designated port districts
Which argument is best supported by the table?

choices.A: Imperialism could operate through unequal treaties and commercial privileges rather than direct annexation alone.
choices.B: Treaty ports made China a settler colony.
choices.C: Foreign commercial privileges strengthened Qing control over tariffs.
choices.D: Industrial-era imperialism avoided legal agreements.

questions.correctAnswerText: Imperialism could operate through unequal treaties and commercial privileges rather than direct annexation alone.
questions.explanation: The table shows commercial access and legal limitations without full territorial annexation.

questions.prompt: Described French colonial school poster, Indochina, c. 1905: “A teacher points to a map of France while Vietnamese children sit at desks beneath a sign reading ‘Work, Obedience, Progress.’ Outside the school window, a village elder watches from a distance.” Which interpretation is best supported by the poster?

choices.A: Colonial education was presented as a tool of cultural assimilation and social discipline.
choices.B: French colonial rule rejected schooling as a political tool.
choices.C: Vietnamese society controlled the French curriculum without interference.
choices.D: Colonial schools were unrelated to imperial ideology.

questions.correctAnswerText: Colonial education was presented as a tool of cultural assimilation and social discipline.
questions.explanation: The poster’s school, map of France, obedience slogan, and colonial setting show assimilationist education.

questions.prompt: Described French colonial school poster, Indochina, c. 1905: “A teacher points to a map of France while Vietnamese children sit at desks beneath a sign reading ‘Work, Obedience, Progress.’ Outside the school window, a village elder watches from a distance.” Which detail most directly supports the claim that the poster promoted obedience to imperial authority?

choices.A: “Vietnamese children”
choices.B: “A map of France”
choices.C: “‘Work, Obedience, Progress’”
choices.D: “A village elder watches”

questions.correctAnswerText: “‘Work, Obedience, Progress’”
questions.explanation: The slogan explicitly promotes obedience and colonial discipline.

questions.prompt: Described French colonial school poster, Indochina, c. 1905: “A teacher points to a map of France while Vietnamese children sit at desks beneath a sign reading ‘Work, Obedience, Progress.’ Outside the school window, a village elder watches from a distance.” Which audience was the poster most likely designed to influence?

choices.A: Colonial officials, settlers, and metropolitan viewers who supported a “civilizing mission”
choices.B: Buddhist monks compiling Silk Road manuscripts
choices.C: Revolutionary delegates in Frankfurt in 1848
choices.D: Merchants negotiating customs duties in Surat

questions.correctAnswerText: Colonial officials, settlers, and metropolitan viewers who supported a “civilizing mission”
questions.explanation: The poster would appeal to viewers who saw colonial schooling as proof of imperial progress.

questions.prompt: Described French colonial school poster, Indochina, c. 1905: “A teacher points to a map of France while Vietnamese children sit at desks beneath a sign reading ‘Work, Obedience, Progress.’ Outside the school window, a village elder watches from a distance.” The poster most directly reflects which broader process?

choices.A: The use of education to impose imperial cultural values and create colonial intermediaries
choices.B: The disappearance of all resistance to colonial rule
choices.C: The replacement of schools by plantation labor in Southeast Asia
choices.D: The end of European cultural influence in Asia before 1900

questions.correctAnswerText: The use of education to impose imperial cultural values and create colonial intermediaries
questions.explanation: The poster shows colonial schooling as a tool for cultural and administrative control.

questions.prompt:
Table: Influenza and wartime movement, simplified global pattern, 1918–1919
Context | Effect on disease spread
Troop camps and ships | Crowded conditions accelerated transmission
Colonial labor units and ports | Connected distant regions to outbreak zones
Postwar demobilization | Returned infected people to home communities
Which conclusion is best supported by the table?

choices.A: Global conflict and mass movement helped spread epidemic disease.
choices.B: Influenza spread only through isolated rural communities.
choices.C: World War I prevented disease from crossing oceans.
choices.D: Demobilization ended disease transmission immediately.

questions.correctAnswerText: Global conflict and mass movement helped spread epidemic disease.
questions.explanation: Troop camps, ships, colonial labor units, ports, and demobilization all facilitated transmission.

questions.prompt:
Table: Influenza and wartime movement, simplified global pattern, 1918–1919
Context | Effect on disease spread
Troop camps and ships | Crowded conditions accelerated transmission
Colonial labor units and ports | Connected distant regions to outbreak zones
Postwar demobilization | Returned infected people to home communities
Which evidence best supports the claim that imperial networks contributed to disease spread?

choices.A: “Postwar demobilization”
choices.B: “Troop camps and ships”
choices.C: “Colonial labor units and ports”
choices.D: “Crowded conditions”

questions.correctAnswerText: “Colonial labor units and ports”
questions.explanation: These show imperial networks linking distant regions to outbreaks.

questions.prompt:
Table: Influenza and wartime movement, simplified global pattern, 1918–1919
Context | Effect on disease spread
Troop camps and ships | Crowded conditions accelerated transmission
Colonial labor units and ports | Connected distant regions to outbreak zones
Postwar demobilization | Returned infected people to home communities
The table most directly illustrates which feature of early twentieth-century globalization?

choices.A: Increased transportation networks could intensify the spread of both people and pathogens.
choices.B: Modern transport eliminated epidemic disease.
choices.C: Disease transmission became unrelated to warfare.
choices.D: Colonial units avoided all contact with global transport systems.

questions.correctAnswerText: Increased transportation networks could intensify the spread of both people and pathogens.
questions.explanation: Ships, ports, troop movements, and demobilization moved both people and disease.

questions.prompt: Described cartoon, Geneva, early 1930s: “A small figure labeled ‘Disarmament Conference’ tries to tie a knot around several giant cannons labeled ‘national pride,’ ‘fear,’ and ‘revenge.’ Diplomats applaud politely while arms manufacturers watch from the balcony.” Which interpretation is best supported by the cartoon?

choices.A: Interwar disarmament efforts faced obstacles from nationalism, insecurity, and economic interests.
choices.B: International conferences ended military competition before World War II.
choices.C: Arms manufacturers opposed all military spending.
choices.D: Interwar diplomacy had no relationship to nationalism.

questions.correctAnswerText: Interwar disarmament efforts faced obstacles from nationalism, insecurity, and economic interests.
questions.explanation: Cannons labeled pride, fear, and revenge, along with arms manufacturers, show obstacles to disarmament.

questions.prompt: Described cartoon, Geneva, early 1930s: “A small figure labeled ‘Disarmament Conference’ tries to tie a knot around several giant cannons labeled ‘national pride,’ ‘fear,’ and ‘revenge.’ Diplomats applaud politely while arms manufacturers watch from the balcony.” Which statement best evaluates the cartoon’s argument?

choices.A: It claims that disarmament failed because citizens rejected nationalism completely.
choices.B: It suggests that polite diplomacy was too weak to overcome the forces driving rearmament.
choices.C: It argues that arms manufacturers had no interest in military policy.
choices.D: It shows that interwar governments had eliminated fear of war.

questions.correctAnswerText: It suggests that polite diplomacy was too weak to overcome the forces driving rearmament.
questions.explanation: The small conference figure and giant cannons imply weak diplomacy facing powerful militarizing forces.

questions.prompt: Originalized speech by Kwame Nkrumah, Ghana, 1957: “Our flag is raised, but the work of freedom begins now. Cocoa and gold must build schools and roads, not merely enrich distant companies. Africa’s liberty will be incomplete while any people remains ruled by another.” What was the most likely purpose of the speech?

choices.A: To reject independence in favor of restored colonial rule
choices.B: To define independence as political sovereignty, economic development, and pan-African solidarity
choices.C: To argue that resource wealth should remain under foreign companies
choices.D: To deny connections between Ghana and other African struggles

questions.correctAnswerText: To define independence as political sovereignty, economic development, and pan-African solidarity
questions.explanation: Nkrumah links the flag to development, resource use, schools, roads, and African liberation.

questions.prompt: Originalized speech by Kwame Nkrumah, Ghana, 1957: “Our flag is raised, but the work of freedom begins now. Cocoa and gold must build schools and roads, not merely enrich distant companies. Africa’s liberty will be incomplete while any people remains ruled by another.” Which phrase best supports the claim that economic development was central to the speaker’s view of independence?

choices.A: “Our flag is raised”
choices.B: “The work of freedom begins now”
choices.C: “Cocoa and gold must build schools and roads”
choices.D: “Any people remains ruled”

questions.correctAnswerText: “Cocoa and gold must build schools and roads”
questions.explanation: This phrase links natural resources to national development.

questions.prompt: Originalized speech by Kwame Nkrumah, Ghana, 1957: “Our flag is raised, but the work of freedom begins now. Cocoa and gold must build schools and roads, not merely enrich distant companies. Africa’s liberty will be incomplete while any people remains ruled by another.” Which broader context best explains the speech?

choices.A: The spread of African decolonization and pan-African political ideas after World War II
choices.B: The restoration of European empires without resistance after 1945
choices.C: The decline of all nationalist movements in Africa after independence
choices.D: The replacement of anticolonial activism by chartered companies

questions.correctAnswerText: The spread of African decolonization and pan-African political ideas after World War II
questions.explanation: The speech connects Ghanaian independence with wider African liberation.

questions.prompt: Described anti-apartheid poster, South Africa, 1980s: “A Black student raises a book in one hand and a broken passbook in the other. Behind the student, factory workers and church leaders march beneath a banner reading ‘Education, Work, Freedom.’ Police vehicles appear at the edge of the poster.” Which interpretation is best supported by the poster?

choices.A: Anti-apartheid activism linked education, labor, religious groups, and resistance to pass laws.
choices.B: Apartheid policies expanded equal citizenship for all South Africans.
choices.C: Anti-apartheid activism avoided mass organizing.
choices.D: The poster supports police enforcement of racial segregation.

questions.correctAnswerText: Anti-apartheid activism linked education, labor, religious groups, and resistance to pass laws.
questions.explanation: The poster includes a student, workers, church leaders, broken passbook, and freedom slogan.

questions.prompt: Described anti-apartheid poster, South Africa, 1980s: “A Black student raises a book in one hand and a broken passbook in the other. Behind the student, factory workers and church leaders march beneath a banner reading ‘Education, Work, Freedom.’ Police vehicles appear at the edge of the poster.” The poster most directly reflects which broader late twentieth-century process?

choices.A: The global decline of human-rights activism
choices.B: The growth of domestic and international movements against racial segregation and minority rule
choices.C: The spread of colonial pass laws to every independent state
choices.D: The disappearance of student politics after 1945

questions.correctAnswerText: The growth of domestic and international movements against racial segregation and minority rule
questions.explanation: The poster reflects broad anti-apartheid mobilization against pass laws and state repression.

questions.prompt:
Table: Global plastic production and waste, simplified pattern
Indicator | c. 1970 | c. 2020
Annual plastic production | Tens of millions of tons | Hundreds of millions of tons
Single-use packaging | Expanding | Widespread in global retail
Plastic waste in waterways | Reported locally | Reported across many ocean basins
Which conclusion is best supported by the table?

choices.A: Plastic production and waste became a global environmental issue tied to consumer markets.
choices.B: Plastic waste disappeared as production increased.
choices.C: Plastic production remained limited to one region after 1970.
choices.D: Single-use packaging declined before global retail expanded.

questions.correctAnswerText: Plastic production and waste became a global environmental issue tied to consumer markets.
questions.explanation: The table shows rising production, single-use packaging, and waste across many ocean basins.

questions.prompt:
Table: Global plastic production and waste, simplified pattern
Indicator | c. 1970 | c. 2020
Annual plastic production | Tens of millions of tons | Hundreds of millions of tons
Single-use packaging | Expanding | Widespread in global retail
Plastic waste in waterways | Reported locally | Reported across many ocean basins
Which evidence best supports the claim that consumer markets contributed to plastic waste?

choices.A: “Reported locally”
choices.B: “Tens of millions of tons”
choices.C: “Single-use packaging”
choices.D: “Ocean basins”

questions.correctAnswerText: “Single-use packaging”
questions.explanation: Single-use packaging directly connects consumer markets to plastic waste.

questions.prompt: Originalized statement by an international environmental coalition, c. 2022: “A bottle used for minutes may travel across borders as waste for decades. Producers, governments, and consumers must be accountable, for the ocean does not sort pollution by flag.” What was the most likely purpose of the statement?

choices.A: To argue that plastic pollution is a shared transnational problem requiring coordinated action
choices.B: To deny that consumer behavior affects the environment
choices.C: To promote unrestricted dumping of plastic waste
choices.D: To return environmental policy to colonial governments

questions.correctAnswerText: To argue that plastic pollution is a shared transnational problem requiring coordinated action
questions.explanation: The statement stresses cross-border waste, producers, governments, consumers, and ocean pollution.

questions.prompt: Originalized statement by an international environmental coalition, c. 2022: “A bottle used for minutes may travel across borders as waste for decades. Producers, governments, and consumers must be accountable, for the ocean does not sort pollution by flag.” Which statement best evaluates the coalition’s argument?

choices.A: It treats pollution as a purely local issue with no global connections.
choices.B: It argues that responsibility for plastic pollution crosses national, corporate, and consumer boundaries.
choices.C: It claims that oceans prevent waste from moving internationally.
choices.D: It rejects public accountability for environmental problems.

questions.correctAnswerText: It argues that responsibility for plastic pollution crosses national, corporate, and consumer boundaries.
questions.explanation: The coalition assigns accountability to producers, governments, and consumers and rejects national boundaries as adequate.

questions.prompt: Originalized statement by an international environmental coalition, c. 2022: “A bottle used for minutes may travel across borders as waste for decades. Producers, governments, and consumers must be accountable, for the ocean does not sort pollution by flag.” The statement most directly reflects which broader feature of globalization?

choices.A: Environmental activism increasingly frames consumption, production, and waste as interconnected global issues.
choices.B: Global consumer culture eliminated environmental criticism.
choices.C: International organizations ended all marine pollution by 2000.
choices.D: Waste became disconnected from trade after 1970.

questions.correctAnswerText: Environmental activism increasingly frames consumption, production, and waste as interconnected global issues.
questions.explanation: The statement links consumer use, international waste movement, oceans, and shared accountability.
